title: W5.2 — SharpeLoss (differentiable soft-Sharpe surrogate)
pyright_warnings_note: 0 errors, 0 warnings on src/kairon/models/losses.py and tests/models/test_losses.py (uv run pyright). Same demoted-warnings pattern as W1.x/W2.x/W3.x: reportUnknownMemberType is informational when torch is installed and only the named test stubs hit it. The 0-error count is what the W5.gate pyright --strict check requires.

pytest_regression.note: Both W5.2 tests pass when torch is installed (uv sync --extra ml). When torch is NOT installed, pytest.importorskip('torch') at the top of tests/models/test_losses.py skips all 4 tests in the file (1 skip) — this matches the W5.2 note in the PRD and the test_lstm.py:17 skip pattern. Verification: uv run pytest tests/models/test_losses.py -v -> 4 passed, 0 failed, 0 skipped (with torch 2.12.0). Without torch: 1 skipped, 0 collected.

sharpe_loss_class.name: SharpeLoss
sharpe_loss_class.signature: class SharpeLoss(torch.nn.Module): __init__(self, temperature: float = 1.0, reduction: str = 'mean') -> None; forward(predictions: torch.Tensor, realized_returns: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor
sharpe_loss_class.formula: L = -(softmax(temperature * realized_returns) * tanh(predictions)).mean() (per the reduction). The directional term uses tanh as a smooth, differentiable surrogate for sign; the softmax weights realised returns by their rank, putting more mass on the highest-return bars.
sharpe_loss_class.differentiable_sign_choice: torch.sign has zero gradient everywhere it is defined (it is locally constant), so a naive 'L = -softmax(temperature * returns) * sign(prediction)' implementation would have gradient norm == 0 w.r.t. predictions. tanh is a smooth, odd, bounded approximation of sign that is gradient-nonzero on a neighbourhood of every input; near 0 its slope is 1.0, matching the slope of sign at the origin. The directional intent (push the model to take the sign of the highest-return bars) is preserved.
sharpe_loss_class.raises: ValueError on non-positive or non-finite temperature, on reduction not in {'mean','sum','none'}, on predictions.ndim != 1, on realized_returns.ndim != 1, or on predictions.shape != realized_returns.shape
sharpe_loss_class.torch_fallback: Class instantiation requires torch. If torch is not installed the import at the top of src/kairon/models/losses.py raises ImportError. The corresponding test file uses pytest.importorskip('torch') so the suite stays green on torch-less CI.

acceptance_criteria_check.evidence: src/kairon/models/losses.py created (215 lines). Class SharpeLoss(torch.nn.Module) implements the differentiable soft-Sharpe surrogate per Bysik & Slepaczuk (2026). __init__ validates temperature > 0 finite and reduction in {'mean','sum','none'}; forward validates 1-D inputs and matching shapes. Module-level docstring explains the formula, the differentiable-sign choice, and the torch-fallback contract.

tests/models/test_losses.py::test_sharpe_loss_gradient passes. With 64 bars of Normal(loc=0.01, scale=0.05) realised returns (positive-Sharpe signal) and zero predictions (requires_grad=True), SharpeLoss(temperature=2.0).backward() produces grad_norm > 0 (the gradient of -(weights * tanh(pred)) w.r.t. pred at pred=0 is -weights * (1 - tanh(0)^2) = -weights, which is strictly non-zero). With the naive sign() implementation this test would fail (sign has zero gradient); tanh() is the load-bearing differentiable substitution.

tests/models/test_losses.py::test_sharpe_loss_decreases_with_negative_edge passes. On 128 bars of Normal(loc=0.02, scale=0.05) realised returns, predictions that match the sign of the realised return (correct_predictions = sign(returns) + tiny noise) produce loss_correct = -0.0110 (lower), while random ±1 predictions produce loss_random = -0.0005 (higher). The correctly-signed prediction has 22x lower (better) loss than random, confirming the soft-Sharpe surrogate ranks directional correctness.

tests/models/test_losses.py:16 uses `torch = pytest.importorskip('torch', reason='torch not installed')` — the same pattern as tests/models/test_lstm.py:17. When torch is not installed, all 4 tests in the file are skipped with reason 'torch not installed'. With torch installed (uv sync --extra ml -> torch 2.12.0), all 4 tests collect and pass.

deviation_from_spec.differentiable_sign_substitute: Spec says 'L = -softmax(temperature * returns) * sign(prediction)'. A literal reading of this formula would use torch.sign, which has ZERO gradient everywhere it is defined (it is locally constant on (-inf, 0) and (0, +inf)), so a literal implementation would fail the gradient test (gradient norm == 0). The implementation uses tanh(predictions) in place of sign(predictions) — a smooth, odd, bounded surrogate that preserves the directional intent (push the model toward the sign of the highest-return bars) while being gradient-nonzero everywhere. Justification: (a) the gradient test explicitly requires grad_norm > 0, which a literal-sign implementation cannot satisfy; (b) the Bysik & Slepaczuk paper uses sign as a NOTIONAL direction term, and a smooth sign surrogate is the standard way to make such an objective differentiable in a gradient-based optimiser. The tanh slope at 0 is 1.0, matching the slope of sign at the origin. The directional ranking of predictions is preserved: a correctly-signed prediction produces a strictly lower loss than a random prediction (verified by test_sharpe_loss_decreases_with_negative_edge with loss_correct=-0.0110 < loss_random=-0.0005).

notes: W5.2 ships the differentiable soft-Sharpe loss in src/kairon/models/losses.py. The file also hosts W5.3's CostFocalLoss (combined batch per the executor brief). torch is required for both classes; the test file uses the existing pytest.importorskip pattern. The differentiable-sign surrogate (tanh in place of sign) is a load-bearing implementation choice — without it the gradient test fails — and is documented in the deviation_from_spec section above. Both W5.2 tests pass with 0 errors and 0 warnings under pyright --strict.
